lesson.menu_title: Pronunciation Lab I — Sounds That Trip Up Brazilian Speakers
lesson.menu_desc: The TH sound, the -ed ending & tricky vowel pairs — sound clearer and more confident — 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 9 — Pronunciation Lab I: Sounds That Trip Up Brazilian Speakers
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Daniela Feitoza | Aula 9 -- Pronunciation Lab I

listenings.text: Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Before your big call on Friday, here are three quick tips for the sounds we worked on. First, the T H sound. Remember to put your tongue lightly between your teeth for words like think, three, and thank you. Do not turn it into an s or a t. Second, the past tense ending, that little e d. In most cases it is just one extra sound, not a full syllable. Worked sounds like workt, not work ed. Only words ending in t or d, like wanted or needed, get the extra syllable. Third, watch the short i and the long e. Live and leave are different words, and so are ship and sheep. Slow down on those. You do not need a perfect American accent. You just need to be clear and easy to understand. Practice these three things for ten minutes a day, and you will sound much more confident on Friday. You can do it. Talk soon.

Hey Daniela, it is Bruno from the Lisbon office. I heard you are working on your pronunciation, so let me tell you a funny story. On my very first call in English, I wanted to say I will send the sheet with the numbers. But I used the short i instead of the long e, and well, it came out as a very different word. Everyone went quiet for a second, and then we all laughed. The lesson I learned is simple. Do not be afraid of mistakes. Native speakers mispronounce things too. What really matters is that you keep talking and you fix the small things little by little. Focus on one sound each week. This week I am working on the T H sound, because in Portuguese we do not have it. By the way, your English is already really good. See you at the team meeting on Thursday.
